{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}} (merged deity) |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = Mishima Shrine |例祭 = |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Yagokoro Ōichi
hiko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine''' (やごころおおいちひこじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kurobe|Q753864|WD=Q753864|1=Kurobe, Toyama}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. Until the Meiji period it was called “Mishima Daimyōjin”; it was given its current name in Meiji, but is still called '''Mishima Shrine''' today. It enshrines {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Sukunabikona}}, and {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} as principal deities, and {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} as a merged deity''Toyama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' (published November 10, 1983 by Toyama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines), p.330.. Spring festival is May 5, annual-year festival is February 20, autumn festival is October 4, and Niiname-sai is November 26. Special rites include the Chinowa rite (June 30) and Jijin Festival (June 25; sacred bamboo is set around the shrine and the event is celebrated with songs and dance). The precinct area is 4,000 tsubo, with 13 million ujiko. === History === There are various theories on foundation: one says it was invited to Sakurai-shō Mikkaichi during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Kōken|Q232955|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}; another says a {{ill|lt=gohei|Q1011372|WD=Q1011372|1=Gohei}} flew from a mountaintop in Karezawa Village to the east (present Karezawa, Kurobe), stayed in Mikkaichi, and when locals enshrined it, three {{ill|lt=shintai statues|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751|1=Idols in Shinto}} appeared, and so it was revered as “Mishima Daimyōjin”''A Historical Walk of Toyama Prefecture'' (Yamakawa Publishing, issued March 31, 2013, 1st ed., 3rd printing), pp.26-27.. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is ranked as a small shrine. From old times it has been deeply revered from nearby and surrounding areas as a deity of abundant harvests, medicine and brewing, fire protection, and foremost divine protection. It is said that in earlier times imperial messengers were sent, offerings and sacred mirrors were dedicated, and rites were carried out with great solemnity. One tradition says the name “Mishima Daimyōjin” comes from {{ill|lt=Sano Genzaemon|Q11385069|WD=Q11385069|1=Sano Genzaemon}} in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, who revered {{ill|lt=Mishima Taisha|Q650772|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Shrine|1=Mishima Taisha}} in Izu, brought a cherry branch from there, and grafted it onto a tree in this shrine’s precinct. The shrine crest is a cherry emblem called “Sakurai-zakura.” Originally it stood about 500 m north of the current site (Kayadō''Commemorative Journal II: Exploring Changes in Architectural Culture in Kurobe City'' (February 20, 2008, Toyama Prefecture Architects Association Kurobe Branch), p.14.), but after shrine buildings, treasures, and old documents were lost to fire in the Nagao clan’s warfare during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}-{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), it was moved to the present site in {{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 8). After major reconstruction of shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=1688|Q7671|WD=Q7671|1=1688}} ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 1), it reverted to its pre-Kamakura shrine name “Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine” after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, and in August {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6) was ranked as the first prefectural shrine in the lower Shin-kawa region''Mishima Community Center New Building Commemoration: Then and Now of Mishima Town in Photos'' (September 1, 1995, Mishima Town Neighborhood Association), p.122.. On {{ill|lt=June 24|Q2665|WD=Q2665|1=June 24}}, {{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 14), Shinmei Shrine in Tenjinshin, {{ill|lt=Ishida Village (Toyama)|Q11587014|WD=Q11587014|lt=Ishida Village|1=Ishida}} (enshrining Amaterasu-Ōmikami), and household earth deities were {{ill|lt=shrine-merged|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=merged|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} (an earth mound was built under the large zelkova at this time)''Toyama Encyclopedia, Vol. 2'' (August 1, 1994, Kita-Nippon Shimbun), p.978.. On {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, {{ill|lt=1942|Q18625|WD=Q18625|1=1942}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 17), the current shrine buildings using cypress from imperial Kiso timber were completed (transfer rites took place the day before, {{ill|lt=October 24|Q2955|WD=Q2955|1=October 24}})''Mishima Community Center New Building Commemoration: Then and Now of Mishima Town in Photos'' (September 1, 1995, Mishima Town Neighborhood Association), p.124.. The precinct was expanded at the same time. Since then, grounds and facilities have been developed progressively. From {{ill|lt=2020|Q25337|WD=Q25337|1=2020}} ({{ill|lt=Reiwa|Q62609017|WD=Q62609017|1=Reiwa era}} 2), work was carried out to replace the copper roof plating, completed in November {{ill|lt=2021|Q49628|WD=Q49628|1=2021}} (Reiwa 3)''{{ill|lt=Kita-Nippon Shimbun|Q11401857|WD=Q11401857|1=The Kitanippon Shimbun}}'', Jan 17, 2022, p.6, “Our Town from the Sky: Mikkaichi, Kurobe City.”. === Facilities === [[File:Mishima-no-Ookeyaki019.jpg|thumb|Mishima’s great zelkova]] The precinct has been designated a Kurobe City {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} since {{ill|lt=July 11|Q2701|WD=Q2701|1=July 11}}, {{ill|lt=1955|Q18577|WD=Q18577|1=1955}}. “Sakurai no Bakefuji” and “Mishima no Ōkeyaki” in detached precinct land (once part of the large original grounds but later separated) are city-designated natural monuments (designated {{ill|lt=February 1|Q2312|WD=Q2312|1=February 1}}, {{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}} and {{ill|lt=September 28|Q2883|WD=Q2883|1=September 28}}, {{ill|lt=1983|Q2434|WD=Q2434|1=1983}}, respectively)[https://www.pref.toyama.jp/documents/14279/07-kurobe.pdf Cultural Properties of Kurobe City No.7] (Toyama Prefecture, accessed December 10, 2022). {{ill|lt=Chicken|Q780|WD=Q780|lt=Chickens|1=Chicken}} are treated as {{ill|lt=divine messengers|Q10444973|WD=Q10444973|1=Shinshi}}, and nearby people reportedly did not eat either meat or eggs. There also remains an {{ill|lt=ema|Q687288|WD=Q687288|1=Ema (Shinto)}} of a chicken dedicated in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1689|Q7674|WD=Q7674|1=1689}}). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山県の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]] [[Category:黒部市の歴史]] [[Category:黒部市の建築物]] [[Category:Mishima–Ōyamazumi cult]]

hiko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine''' (やごころおおいちひこじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kurobe|Q753864|WD=Q753864|1=Kurobe, Toyama}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. Until the Meiji period it was called “Mishima Daimyōjin”; it was given its current name in Meiji, but is still called '''Mishima Shrine''' today. It enshrines {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Sukunabikona}}, and {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} as principal deities, and {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} as a merged deity''Toyama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' (published November 10, 1983 by Toyama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines), p.330.. Spring festival is May 5, annual-year festival is February 20, autumn festival is October 4, and Niiname-sai is November 26. Special rites include the Chinowa rite (June 30) and Jijin Festival (June 25; sacred bamboo is set around the shrine and the event is celebrated with songs and dance). The precinct area is 4,000 tsubo, with 13 million ujiko. === History === There are various theories on foundation: one says it was invited to Sakurai-shō Mikkaichi during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Kōken|Q232955|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}; another says a {{ill|lt=gohei|Q1011372|WD=Q1011372|1=Gohei}} flew from a mountaintop in Karezawa Village to the east (present Karezawa, Kurobe), stayed in Mikkaichi, and when locals enshrined it, three {{ill|lt=shintai statues|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751|1=Idols in Shinto}} appeared, and so it was revered as “Mishima Daimyōjin”''A Historical Walk of Toyama Prefecture'' (Yamakawa Publishing, issued March 31, 2013, 1st ed., 3rd printing), pp.26-27.. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is ranked as a small shrine. From old times it has been deeply revered from nearby and surrounding areas as a deity of abundant harvests, medicine and brewing, fire protection, and foremost divine protection. It is said that in earlier times imperial messengers were sent, offerings and sacred mirrors were dedicated, and rites were carried out with great solemnity. One tradition says the name “Mishima Daimyōjin” comes from {{ill|lt=Sano Genzaemon|Q11385069|WD=Q11385069|1=Sano Genzaemon}} in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, who revered {{ill|lt=Mishima Taisha|Q650772|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Shrine|1=Mishima Taisha}} in Izu, brought a cherry branch from there, and grafted it onto a tree in this shrine’s precinct. The shrine crest is a cherry emblem called “Sakurai-zakura.” Originally it stood about 500 m north of the current site (Kayadō''Commemorative Journal II: Exploring Changes in Architectural Culture in Kurobe City'' (February 20, 2008, Toyama Prefecture Architects Association Kurobe Branch), p.14.), but after shrine buildings, treasures, and old documents were lost to fire in the Nagao clan’s warfare during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}-{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), it was moved to the present site in {{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 8). After major reconstruction of shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=1688|Q7671|WD=Q7671|1=1688}} ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 1), it reverted to its pre-Kamakura shrine name “Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine” after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, and in August {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6) was ranked as the first prefectural shrine in the lower Shin-kawa region''Mishima Community Center New Building Commemoration: Then and Now of Mishima Town in Photos'' (September 1, 1995, Mishima Town Neighborhood Association), p.122.. On {{ill|lt=June 24|Q2665|WD=Q2665|1=June 24}}, {{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 14), Shinmei Shrine in Tenjinshin, {{ill|lt=Ishida Village (Toyama)|Q11587014|WD=Q11587014|lt=Ishida Village|1=Ishida}} (enshrining Amaterasu-Ōmikami), and household earth deities were {{ill|lt=shrine-merged|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=merged|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} (an earth mound was built under the large zelkova at this time)''Toyama Encyclopedia, Vol. 2'' (August 1, 1994, Kita-Nippon Shimbun), p.978.. On {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, {{ill|lt=1942|Q18625|WD=Q18625|1=1942}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 17), the current shrine buildings using cypress from imperial Kiso timber were completed (transfer rites took place the day before, {{ill|lt=October 24|Q2955|WD=Q2955|1=October 24}})''Mishima Community Center New Building Commemoration: Then and Now of Mishima Town in Photos'' (September 1, 1995, Mishima Town Neighborhood Association), p.124.. The precinct was expanded at the same time. Since then, grounds and facilities have been developed progressively. From {{ill|lt=2020|Q25337|WD=Q25337|1=2020}} ({{ill|lt=Reiwa|Q62609017|WD=Q62609017|1=Reiwa era}} 2), work was carried out to replace the copper roof plating, completed in November {{ill|lt=2021|Q49628|WD=Q49628|1=2021}} (Reiwa 3)''{{ill|lt=Kita-Nippon Shimbun|Q11401857|WD=Q11401857|1=The Kitanippon Shimbun}}'', Jan 17, 2022, p.6, “Our Town from the Sky: Mikkaichi, Kurobe City.”. === Facilities === [[File:Mishima-no-Ookeyaki019.jpg|thumb|Mishima’s great zelkova]] The precinct has been designated a Kurobe City {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} since {{ill|lt=July 11|Q2701|WD=Q2701|1=July 11}}, {{ill|lt=1955|Q18577|WD=Q18577|1=1955}}. “Sakurai no Bakefuji” and “Mishima no Ōkeyaki” in detached precinct land (once part of the large original grounds but later separated) are city-designated natural monuments (designated {{ill|lt=February 1|Q2312|WD=Q2312|1=February 1}}, {{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}} and {{ill|lt=September 28|Q2883|WD=Q2883|1=September 28}}, {{ill|lt=1983|Q2434|WD=Q2434|1=1983}}, respectively)[https://www.pref.toyama.jp/documents/14279/07-kurobe.pdf Cultural Properties of Kurobe City No.7] (Toyama Prefecture, accessed December 10, 2022). {{ill|lt=Chicken|Q780|WD=Q780|lt=Chickens|1=Chicken}} are treated as {{ill|lt=divine messengers|Q10444973|WD=Q10444973|1=Shinshi}}, and nearby people reportedly did not eat either meat or eggs. There also remains an {{ill|lt=ema|Q687288|WD=Q687288|1=Ema (Shinto)}} of a chicken dedicated in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1689|Q7674|WD=Q7674|1=1689}}). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]] [[Category:黒部市の歴史]] [[Category:黒部市の建築物]] [[Category:Mishima–Ōyamazumi cult]]

'language': 'ja'} [[JA:八心大市比古神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11391120}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine |画像 = [[File:Yagokoro-Ooichihiko-Jinja005.jpg|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kurobe|Q753864|WD=Q753864|1=Kurobe, Toyama}} Mikkaichi 1036-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyamazumi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}} (merged deity) |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = Mishima Shrine |例祭 = |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Yagokoro Ōichi
hiko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine''' (やごころおおいちひこじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kurobe|Q753864|WD=Q753864|1=Kurobe, Toyama}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. Until the Meiji period it was called “Mishima Daimyōjin”; it was given its current name in Meiji, but is still called '''Mishima Shrine''' today. It enshrines {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Sukunabikona}}, and {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Kami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} as principal deities, and {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} as a merged deity''Toyama Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' (published November 10, 1983 by Toyama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines), p.330.. Spring festival is May 5, annual-year festival is February 20, autumn festival is October 4, and Niiname-sai is November 26. Special rites include the Chinowa rite (June 30) and Jijin Festival (June 25; sacred bamboo is set around the shrine and the event is celebrated with songs and dance). The precinct area is 4,000 tsubo, with 13 million ujiko. === History === There are various theories on foundation: one says it was invited to Sakurai-shō Mikkaichi during the reign of {{ill|lt=Empress Kōken|Q232955|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}; another says a {{ill|lt=gohei|Q1011372|WD=Q1011372|1=Gohei}} flew from a mountaintop in Karezawa Village to the east (present Karezawa, Kurobe), stayed in Mikkaichi, and when locals enshrined it, three {{ill|lt=shintai statues|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751|1=Idols in Shinto}} appeared, and so it was revered as “Mishima Daimyōjin”''A Historical Walk of Toyama Prefecture'' (Yamakawa Publishing, issued March 31, 2013, 1st ed., 3rd printing), pp.26-27.. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is ranked as a small shrine. From old times it has been deeply revered from nearby and surrounding areas as a deity of abundant harvests, medicine and brewing, fire protection, and foremost divine protection. It is said that in earlier times imperial messengers were sent, offerings and sacred mirrors were dedicated, and rites were carried out with great solemnity. One tradition says the name “Mishima Daimyōjin” comes from {{ill|lt=Sano Genzaemon|Q11385069|WD=Q11385069|1=Sano Genzaemon}} in the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, who revered {{ill|lt=Mishima Taisha|Q650772|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Shrine|1=Mishima Taisha}} in Izu, brought a cherry branch from there, and grafted it onto a tree in this shrine’s precinct. The shrine crest is a cherry emblem called “Sakurai-zakura.” Originally it stood about 500 m north of the current site (Kayadō''Commemorative Journal II: Exploring Changes in Architectural Culture in Kurobe City'' (February 20, 2008, Toyama Prefecture Architects Association Kurobe Branch), p.14.), but after shrine buildings, treasures, and old documents were lost to fire in the Nagao clan’s warfare during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era ({{ill|lt=1558|Q6601|WD=Q6601|1=1558}}-{{ill|lt=1570|Q6659|WD=Q6659|1=1570}}), it was moved to the present site in {{ill|lt=1580|Q6712|WD=Q6712|1=1580}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 8). After major reconstruction of shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=1688|Q7671|WD=Q7671|1=1688}} ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 1), it reverted to its pre-Kamakura shrine name “Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine” after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, and in August {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6) was ranked as the first prefectural shrine in the lower Shin-kawa region''Mishima Community Center New Building Commemoration: Then and Now of Mishima Town in Photos'' (September 1, 1995, Mishima Town Neighborhood Association), p.122.. On {{ill|lt=June 24|Q2665|WD=Q2665|1=June 24}}, {{ill|lt=1925|Q18107|WD=Q18107|1=1925}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 14), Shinmei Shrine in Tenjinshin, {{ill|lt=Ishida Village (Toyama)|Q11587014|WD=Q11587014|lt=Ishida Village|1=Ishida}} (enshrining Amaterasu-Ōmikami), and household earth deities were {{ill|lt=shrine-merged|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=merged|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} (an earth mound was built under the large zelkova at this time)''Toyama Encyclopedia, Vol. 2'' (August 1, 1994, Kita-Nippon Shimbun), p.978.. On {{ill|lt=October 25|Q2954|WD=Q2954|1=October 25}}, {{ill|lt=1942|Q18625|WD=Q18625|1=1942}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 17), the current shrine buildings using cypress from imperial Kiso timber were completed (transfer rites took place the day before, {{ill|lt=October 24|Q2955|WD=Q2955|1=October 24}})''Mishima Community Center New Building Commemoration: Then and Now of Mishima Town in Photos'' (September 1, 1995, Mishima Town Neighborhood Association), p.124.. The precinct was expanded at the same time. Since then, grounds and facilities have been developed progressively. From {{ill|lt=2020|Q25337|WD=Q25337|1=2020}} ({{ill|lt=Reiwa|Q62609017|WD=Q62609017|1=Reiwa era}} 2), work was carried out to replace the copper roof plating, completed in November {{ill|lt=2021|Q49628|WD=Q49628|1=2021}} (Reiwa 3)''{{ill|lt=Kita-Nippon Shimbun|Q11401857|WD=Q11401857|1=The Kitanippon Shimbun}}'', Jan 17, 2022, p.6, “Our Town from the Sky: Mikkaichi, Kurobe City.”. === Facilities === [[File:Mishima-no-Ookeyaki019.jpg|thumb|Mishima’s great zelkova]] The precinct has been designated a Kurobe City {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}} since {{ill|lt=July 11|Q2701|WD=Q2701|1=July 11}}, {{ill|lt=1955|Q18577|WD=Q18577|1=1955}}. “Sakurai no Bakefuji” and “Mishima no Ōkeyaki” in detached precinct land (once part of the large original grounds but later separated) are city-designated natural monuments (designated {{ill|lt=February 1|Q2312|WD=Q2312|1=February 1}}, {{ill|lt=1965|Q2650|WD=Q2650|1=1965}} and {{ill|lt=September 28|Q2883|WD=Q2883|1=September 28}}, {{ill|lt=1983|Q2434|WD=Q2434|1=1983}}, respectively)[https://www.pref.toyama.jp/documents/14279/07-kurobe.pdf Cultural Properties of Kurobe City No.7] (Toyama Prefecture, accessed December 10, 2022). {{ill|lt=Chicken|Q780|WD=Q780|lt=Chickens|1=Chicken}} are treated as {{ill|lt=divine messengers|Q10444973|WD=Q10444973|1=Shinshi}}, and nearby people reportedly did not eat either meat or eggs. There also remains an {{ill|lt=ema|Q687288|WD=Q687288|1=Ema (Shinto)}} of a chicken dedicated in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1689|Q7674|WD=Q7674|1=1689}}). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yagokorōoichihiko Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:富山県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]] [[Category:黒部市の歴史]] [[Category:黒部市の建築物]] [[Category:Mishima–Ōyamazumi cult]]
